content.text: What Are Operators and Expressions

An operator is a symbol that performs a computation between one or more operands. An expression is any combination of values, variables, operators, and function calls that Python can evaluate to produce a single value. Operators are the vocabulary of computation; expressions are the sentences built from that vocabulary.

Python provides arithmetic operators (+, -, *, /, //, %, **), comparison operators (==, !=, <, <=, >, >=), logical operators (and, or, not), bitwise operators (&, |, ^, ~, <<, >>), identity operators (is, is not), membership operators (in, not in), and assignment operators (=, +=, -=, *=, etc.). Python 3.8 added the walrus operator (:=), and Python 3.9 added the dict merge operator (|).

Every operator in Python can be overloaded by implementing dunder (double-underscore) methods on a class. This is how numpy arrays support +, -, *, / element-wise, how pandas DataFrames support comparison operators, and how custom objects can behave like numbers or sequences. Understanding operators is therefore both a foundation for writing expressions and a key to understanding how third-party libraries work.
Why Operators and Expressions Matter

Operators appear in virtually every line of substantive Python code. Choosing the right operator can make code dramatically more readable. Compare x == None versus x is None: both test for None, but the second is correct (None is a singleton and should be compared by identity), and any experienced Python reader recognises the intent instantly. Similarly, using // for integer division instead of int(a / b) is more readable and avoids a subtle float-rounding bug.

Operator precedence errors are a common source of bugs. When you write not a and b or c, do you mean not (a and b or c), (not a) and (b or c), or something else? Understanding precedence — and using parentheses liberally to express intent — prevents these bugs entirely. The Python documentation has a full precedence table; knowing the broad ordering (power > unary > multiply > add > compare > boolean) is enough for daily use.
Benefits
Arithmetic operators work on ints, floats, complex numbers, and Decimal — Python selects the appropriate computation automatically.
Augmented assignment operators (+=, -=, etc.) are both concise and, for mutable objects like lists, in-place (avoiding a copy).
Chained comparisons (1 < x < 10) evaluate each side only once, matching mathematical notation exactly.
Identity operators (is, is not) provide object-identity testing, separate from value equality.
Membership operators (in, not in) work on strings, lists, dicts (keys), sets, and any object that implements __contains__.
Bitwise operators enable efficient integer-flag patterns and low-level data manipulation without external libraries.
The walrus operator (:=) eliminates the pattern of computing a value, assigning it, and then checking it in a loop or condition.
Trade-offs

Python's operators are higher-level than C's: integer division with / always returns a float, and integer exponentiation with ** can return arbitrarily large integers. This is convenient but can cause surprises for programmers coming from C. Always use // for integer division when you need an integer result.

Boolean operators and, or are short-circuit: and returns the first falsy operand, or returns the first truthy operand, or the last operand if none match. This means x or default is a common idiom for providing a default value, but it fails when x is a valid falsy value (0, empty string, False). Use x if x is not None else default instead when you need to distinguish None from other falsy values.

Augmented assignment on immutable objects (strings, tuples, frozensets) is a rebinding, not an in-place mutation. s += 'a' creates a new string and rebinds s. For mutable objects (list, dict, set), augmented assignment delegates to in-place mutation methods (__iadd__, __isub__, etc.), which is O(1) or amortised O(1).
How Operators Work
Arithmetic operators

Python's arithmetic operators perform standard mathematical operations. Division (/) always produces a float. Floor division (//) truncates toward negative infinity (not toward zero: -7 // 2 == -4, not -3). Modulo (%) returns the remainder; for negative numbers, the result has the same sign as the divisor (which differs from C's % operator). Exponentiation (**) is right-associative: 2 ** 3 ** 2 == 2 ** 9 == 512.
Comparison operators

Comparison operators return bool values. Python supports chained comparisons: a < b < c is equivalent to a < b and b < c, but b is evaluated only once. This is syntactic sugar that Python evaluates using short-circuit logic: if a < b is False, c is never tested.
Logical operators

and and or do not return booleans — they return one of their operands. and returns the first falsy value, or the last value if all are truthy. or returns the first truthy value, or the last value if all are falsy. This enables idioms like value = user_input or 'default'. not always returns a bool.
Bitwise operators

Bitwise operators treat integers as binary numbers and operate bit-by-bit. & (AND), | (OR), ^ (XOR), ~ (NOT / complement), << (left shift), >> (right shift). These are used for flag sets, permission masks, hash functions, and low-level protocol parsing. In Python, integers have arbitrary precision, so there is no overflow from shifting.
Identity and membership

is tests whether two names reference the same object in memory (same id()). == tests whether two objects have equal value (delegating to __eq__). For None, True, and False, always use is/is not. For numbers, strings, and collections, use == unless you specifically need identity. in calls __contains__ on the right operand; for dicts, it checks keys; for sets, it is O(1); for lists, O(n).
Operator Precedence Diagram

attrs.alt: Python operator precedence table from highest (parentheses) to lowest (assignment), showing each group with examples

content.text: When to Use Each Operator Group
Group
Operators
Typical use
Arithmetic
Math, string/list repetition, floor division for integer results
Comparison
Filtering, sorting keys, conditional branching
Identity
is  is not
Testing for None, True, False; checking object identity
Membership
in  not in
Checking if a value exists in a collection
Logical
and  or  not
Combining conditions; short-circuit default values
Bitwise
Permission flags, bitmask operations, low-level data
Augmented assign
In-place update; += on list calls extend
Walrus
Assign and test in one expression (loops, comprehensions)
How to Implement
Arithmetic and comparison

# --- Arithmetic operators ---
a, b = 17, 5

print(a + b)    # 22  addition
print(a - b)    # 12  subtraction
print(a * b)    # 85  multiplication
print(a / b)    # 3.4 true division — always float
print(a // b)   # 3   floor division — truncates toward -inf
print(a % b)    # 2   modulo (remainder)
print(a ** b)   # 1419857  exponentiation

# Floor division vs truncation for negatives
print(-7 // 2)   # -4 (floor toward -inf)
print(int(-7/2)) # -3 (truncate toward zero)
print(-7 % 2)    #  1 (result has sign of divisor in Python)

# Integer division use case: pagination
items = 107
per_page = 10
pages = (items + per_page - 1) // per_page   # ceiling division: 11
# Or: import math; math.ceil(items / per_page)

# Large integers (Python has arbitrary precision)
factorial_20 = 1
for i in range(1, 21):
    factorial_20 *= i
print(factorial_20)   # 2432902008176640000

# --- Comparison and chaining ---
x = 7
print(1 < x < 10)        # True — chained comparison
print(0 <= x <= 100)     # True
print(x == 7 != 0)       # True — chained: (x == 7) and (7 != 0)

# Comparisons return bool
result = (3 + 4) == 7    # True
print(type(result))       # <class 'bool'>

# Sorting by multiple keys with tuples
records = [("Bob", 25), ("Alice", 30), ("Alice", 25)]
records.sort()   # sorts by first element, then second
print(records)   # [('Alice', 25), ('Alice', 30), ('Bob', 25)]
Logical, bitwise, identity, membership, walrus

# --- Logical operators (short-circuit, return operand not bool) ---
print(0 or "default")      # "default" — 0 is falsy
print("value" or "other")  # "value" — truthy, stops here
print(None and "x")        # None — None is falsy, returns None
print(True and "x")        # "x" — True is truthy, returns last
print(not 0)               # True
print(not "hello")         # False

# Common idiom: provide a default only when value is None
user_name = None
name = user_name if user_name is not None else "Anonymous"
# Note: don't use `user_name or "Anonymous"` — fails for "" (empty string)

# --- Identity operators ---
x = [1, 2, 3]
y = [1, 2, 3]
z = x

print(x == y)    # True  — equal values
print(x is y)    # False — different objects
print(x is z)    # True  — same object

# None, True, False are singletons — use is
value = None
if value is None:      # correct
    pass
if value == None:      # works but misleading (can be overridden)
    pass

# --- Membership operators ---
fruits = {"apple", "banana", "cherry"}
print("apple" in fruits)       # True  — O(1) for set
print("grape" not in fruits)   # True

text = "hello world"
print("world" in text)         # True — substring check for str

config = {"host": "localhost", "port": 5432}
print("port" in config)        # True — checks keys for dict

# --- Bitwise operators ---
READ   = 0b001   # 1
WRITE  = 0b010   # 2
EXEC   = 0b100   # 4

user_perms = READ | WRITE     # 0b011 = 3  — grant read and write
has_read  = bool(user_perms & READ)    # True
has_exec  = bool(user_perms & EXEC)    # False
user_perms |= EXEC                     # add execute permission
user_perms &= ~WRITE                   # remove write permission

# Bitwise shift
print(1 << 4)   # 16  (shift left = multiply by 2^n)
print(32 >> 2)  # 8   (shift right = divide by 2^n)

# --- Walrus operator (Python 3.8+) ---
import re

# Without walrus
m = re.search(r'\d+', "price: 42 USD")
if m:
    print(m.group())   # 42

# With walrus — compute and test in one line
if m := re.search(r'\d+', "price: 42 USD"):
    print(m.group())   # 42

# Walrus in while loop (read until empty)
data = iter(["line1", "line2", "", "line3"])
while chunk := next(data, ""):
    print(chunk)   # prints line1, line2 (stops at empty string)

# Walrus in comprehension — avoid computing expensive(x) twice
results = [y for x in range(20) if (y := x ** 2) > 50]
Augmented assignment and operator overloading

# --- Augmented assignment ---
count = 0
count += 1    # equivalent to count = count + 1
count *= 2
count //= 3

# On lists, += calls extend (in-place), not __add__ (new list)
lst = [1, 2]
original_id = id(lst)
lst += [3, 4]           # in-place via __iadd__
print(id(lst) == original_id)  # True — same list object!

lst2 = lst + [5, 6]     # creates a NEW list
print(id(lst2) == original_id) # False

# On strings (immutable), += always creates a new string
s = "hello"
s += " world"   # rebinding: s now points to a new string

# --- Operator overloading in practice (how libraries use it) ---
# numpy example (conceptual — numpy not required to understand)
class Vector:
    def __init__(self, x, y):
        self.x, self.y = x, y

    def __add__(self, other):
        return Vector(self.x + other.x, self.y + other.y)

    def __mul__(self, scalar):
        return Vector(self.x * scalar, self.y * scalar)

    def __repr__(self):
        return f"Vector({self.x}, {self.y})"

    def __eq__(self, other):
        return self.x == other.x and self.y == other.y

v1 = Vector(1, 2)
v2 = Vector(3, 4)
print(v1 + v2)     # Vector(4, 6)
print(v1 * 3)      # Vector(3, 6)
print(v1 == Vector(1, 2))  # True

# --- Division operator summary ---
print(7 / 2)    # 3.5  float division
print(7 // 2)   # 3    floor division
print(7 % 2)    # 1    modulo
q, r = divmod(7, 2)   # (3, 1) — quotient and remainder in one call
Real-world Example: Permission System

Bitwise operators are the standard way to implement permission systems. Each permission is a power of two, and a user's permissions are stored as an integer bitmask. Checking, granting, and revoking permissions all use bitwise operations.

from enum import IntFlag

class Permission(IntFlag):
    NONE  = 0
    READ  = 1
    WRITE = 2
    EXEC  = 4
    ADMIN = 8
    ALL   = READ | WRITE | EXEC | ADMIN   # 15

# Grant permissions
user_perm = Permission.READ | Permission.WRITE    # = 3

# Check a permission
def can(perm: Permission, check: Permission) -> bool:
    return bool(perm & check)

print(can(user_perm, Permission.READ))    # True
print(can(user_perm, Permission.EXEC))    # False

# Grant a permission
user_perm |= Permission.EXEC
print(can(user_perm, Permission.EXEC))    # True

# Revoke a permission
user_perm &= ~Permission.WRITE
print(can(user_perm, Permission.WRITE))   # False

# All permissions
admin_perm = Permission.ALL
for p in [Permission.READ, Permission.WRITE, Permission.EXEC, Permission.ADMIN]:
    print(f"{p.name}: {can(admin_perm, p)}")   # all True

# Using comparison operators in data pipelines
scores = [85, 42, 93, 67, 78, 55, 91]
threshold = 70
passing = [s for s in scores if s >= threshold]
mean = sum(passing) / len(passing) if passing else 0
print(f"Passing scores: {passing}")
print(f"Pass rate: {len(passing)/len(scores):.0%}")
print(f"Average passing score: {mean:.1f}")
Common Mistakes
Using == instead of is for None checks: if x == None fails when x has a custom __eq__ that returns True for None.
Expecting / to return an integer: 7 / 2 == 3.5 in Python 3. Use 7 // 2 for integer division.
Relying on x or default when x can legitimately be 0, False, or empty string — use x if x is not None else default.
Forgetting operator precedence: not x == y means not (x == y), but the intent may have been (not x) == y. Use parentheses.
Chaining comparisons incorrectly: a != b != c checks a != b and b != c, not that all three are distinct.
Using is to compare integers outside the small-int cache (-5 to 256): x is 1000 may be True or False depending on Python version and context.
Augmented assignment on a tuple: t += (4,) creates a new tuple and rebinds t, not an in-place mutation. The tuple content is unchanged.
Best Practices
Use is / is not for None, True, and False — never ==.
Use // explicitly for integer division — do not rely on int(a/b), which rounds toward zero and involves a float intermediate.
Parenthesise complex boolean expressions to make precedence explicit: (a > 0 and b < 10) or c is not None.
Prefer x if x is not None else default over x or default when x can be falsy but valid.
Use augmented assignment (+=, |=, etc.) for in-place updates on mutable objects.
Use divmod(a, b) when you need both quotient and remainder.
Use IntFlag or IntEnum from the enum module to give names to bitwise-flag values.
Learn the precedence table well enough to know when parentheses are necessary; add them liberally when there is any doubt.
Performance and Scalability

Integer arithmetic in Python is implemented in C and is fast for values that fit in a machine word. For very large integers (hundreds of digits), Python switches to big-integer arithmetic, which is slower. For performance-critical numeric code, use numpy, which offloads arithmetic to compiled C or Fortran code.

Boolean short-circuit evaluation means and and or can be faster than their non-short-circuit equivalents: if cheap_check and expensive_check(), the expensive check is skipped when the cheap one fails. Order your conditions from cheapest to most expensive.

Bitwise operations on integers are among the fastest operations Python can perform — they map directly to CPU instructions. Bitmask-based permission checks are faster than string comparisons or set membership for the same flags.
Tooling and Ecosystem
operator module (stdlib): itemgetter, attrgetter, methodcaller — function versions of Python operators usable as keys.
functools.reduce with operator.add, operator.mul: functional-style aggregation without lambda.
math module: math.floor(), math.ceil(), math.pow(), math.log() — mathematical functions beyond basic operators.
decimal.Decimal: arbitrary-precision decimal arithmetic — use when floating-point rounding is unacceptable.
fractions.Fraction: exact rational arithmetic — 1/3 is exactly 1/3, not 0.333...
numpy: vectorised operators on arrays; + on two arrays adds element-wise instead of list concatenation.
mypy: checks that operator overloads are consistent with type annotations.
Comparison with Other Languages
Concept
Python
JavaScript
C / C++
Integer division
Math.floor(7/2) = 3
Power
2 ** 8 (ES7) or Math.pow
pow(2, 8) or no **
Integer overflow
Never (arbitrary size)
Silently wraps
Undefined behaviour
True division
7 / 2 = 3.5 always
7.0 / 2.0 (needs cast)
Logical and/or
Returns operand
Returns operand
Returns bool
Ternary
x if c else y
c ? x : y
c ? x : y
Walrus
no equivalent
= in conditions (C-style)
Security and Reliability
Never use eval() to compute arithmetic from user input — it executes arbitrary Python code. Use ast.literal_eval() for safe literal parsing, or a purpose-built expression evaluator.
Integer overflow cannot occur in Python (arbitrary precision), but floating-point overflow (inf) and underflow (0.0) can. Use math.isfinite() to check.
Comparison operators with NaN: float('nan') == float('nan') is False. Use math.isnan() to detect NaN values.
Bitwise operations on very large integers are safe but slow; avoid them in performance-critical paths with large numbers.
Mental Model

Think of operators as the grammar of Python expressions. Just as in English you need to know which words are verbs and which are nouns to parse a sentence, in Python you need to know operator precedence to know how an expression groups. When in doubt, add parentheses — they cost nothing and communicate intent.

Think of logical operators (and, or) not as boolean combinators but as selectors: and selects the first falsy value or the last value; or selects the first truthy value or the last value. This mental model explains idioms like default = config.get('key') or 'fallback' and value = a and b and c.
Key Takeaways
/ always returns float; // returns an integer (floor division); use // when you need an integer result.
** is exponentiation and is right-associative (2**3**2 == 512, not 64).
is / is not test object identity; == tests value equality. Use is for None, True, and False.
and / or are short-circuit and return one of their operands — not necessarily a bool.
Chained comparisons (1 < x < 10) evaluate each side once and are idiomatic Python.
Bitwise operators (&, |, ^, ~, <<, >>) are fast and perfect for flag-based permission systems.
The walrus operator (:=) assigns and returns a value in a single expression — useful in while loops and comprehension filters.
Operator precedence: power > unary > multiply > add > bitwise shift > bitwise AND/XOR/OR > comparisons > boolean not/and/or > ternary > walrus > assignment.
